title: Modern Angle Chair
summary: A compact modern dining/accent chair with subtly raked back legs and a solid panel seat. Pocket-hole joinery; the 5° angles do the styling.
description:
This is a small modern chair — dining-height, clean-lined — that gets its look from a few gentle angles rather than curves or complex joinery. The back legs are raked 5° so they splay slightly for stability and style, the side aprons pick up that same 5° so the seat frame stays level on angled legs, and the back rest kicks back 15° for comfort. None of these are hard cuts, but they're what separate this from a plain four-legs-and-a-box stool.

All the joinery is pocket screws, and the build sequence is worth following because it keeps things square while you fight angled parts. Start with the front legs and front apron, use a scrap of 1/4" plywood as a spacer to inset the aprons 1/4" from the leg faces, then work around: side aprons to front legs, then (resting a level on the side apron) side aprons to the back legs, then the back apron. Corner bracing goes in regardless — the plan adds it 'anyway' even with pocket screws, and on a chair that takes real weight, that's the right instinct.

The seat is a single 18" x 18" panel — a store-bought pine project panel, or 1x stock joined edge to edge with pocket holes. Notch the back corners 1-1/2" to clear the back legs. The plan's builder found the 5° back-notch angle unnecessary and skipped it with good results, so a square notch is fine. Attach the seat from underneath through the aprons.

tools.note: Every joint on the chair is a pocket screw.
Rest it on the side apron to keep the seat frame level while attaching to raked back legs.

materials.name: 1x2 and 1x4 scrap (15 in)
materials.note: Back rest rails and corner bracing.

materials.name: Pine project panel, 18 in x 18 in
materials.note: Seat — or join 1x stock to 18" x 18".

cutList.note: Ends mitered at 5° off square, short point to long point.
One end mitered down 5° off square.

steps.title: Build the chair frame
steps.body: Start with the front legs and pocket-screw the front apron to them, using a scrap of 1/4" plywood as a spacer to inset the apron 1/4" from the leg faces. Attach the side aprons to the front legs the same way. Then rest a level on a side apron as you fasten it to the back legs, so the seat frame stays level even though the back legs are raked 5°. Finally attach the back apron. If you don't have a jig, predrill and countersink — and add corner bracing.

steps.title: Add corner bracing
steps.body: Cut corner braces from scrap 1x3 and fit them into the corners of the seat frame. Add them even if you used pocket screws — on a chair that takes a person's full weight and side loads, the extra bracing is cheap insurance against a joint working loose.

steps.title: Build the back rest
steps.body: Build the back rest by attaching the top and bottom rails to the two 15°-mitered uprights. Pocket screws or predrilled-and-countersunk screws both work. Center the rails on the uprights, leaving a 3/8" gap on the front and back. The 15° kick is what makes the chair comfortable to lean into.

steps.title: Make and attach the seat
steps.body:
Cut the seat to 18" x 18" (a project panel, or join 1x stock edge to edge with pocket holes). Notch the back corners 1-1/2" x 1-1/2" to clear the back legs — a square notch is fine, the 5° back-angle notch is optional and can be skipped.

Attach the seat from underneath through the aprons with pocket screws, or screw down through the top into the aprons. Fill holes, sand, and finish.
materials: Pine project panel, 18 in x 18 in

images.alt: Modern wooden chair with raked back legs and a solid panel seat
